activities.title: Where does money come from?
activities.content:
Before you can budget, you need to understand your income clearly. In New Zealand, income comes in many forms — and each has different tax treatment, reliability, and growth potential.

**Active income:** You trade time for money (wages, salary, freelance work)
**Passive income:** Money works for you (rent, dividends, interest)

Most young New Zealanders start with active income only. The goal over time is to build passive income streams that reduce dependence on a single job.
activities.notePrompt: What is your current primary source of income? Is it active or passive?

activities.title: PAYE — how your wages are taxed
activities.content:
In New Zealand, most employees pay tax through **PAYE (Pay As You Earn)**. Your employer deducts tax before paying you, so you never touch the gross amount.

**NZ income tax rates (2025):**
- Up to $14,000: 10.5%
- $14,001 – $48,000: 17.5%
- $48,001 – $70,000: 30%
- $70,001 – $180,000: 33%
- Over $180,000: 39%

If you earn $30,000/year: first $14,000 taxed at 10.5%, remaining $16,000 at 17.5%.
Always budget using your **net (take-home) pay**, not the gross figure.
activities.notePrompt: If a job advertises $25/hour for 20 hours/week, what is the approximate gross weekly income? Why would your actual take-home be less?

activities.title: KiwiSaver and employer contributions
activities.content:
**KiwiSaver** is NZ's voluntary workplace savings scheme. Understanding it is essential for budgeting:

- Your contribution: 3%, 4%, 6%, 8%, or 10% of gross pay (you choose)
- Employer contribution: minimum 3% of gross pay (legislated)
- Government: up to $521/year (member tax credit if you contribute enough)

**Important:** KiwiSaver is deducted BEFORE you receive your pay — like tax. When budgeting, your take-home is after tax AND after KiwiSaver.

At minimum 3%: on $600/week gross, you contribute $18, employer adds $18, government adds ~$10/week. That is $46/week building in the background.
activities.notePrompt: Are you enrolled in KiwiSaver? What contribution rate do you use, or would you choose?

questions.question: Mia earns $22/hour and works 25 hours/week. Her PAYE tax rate is 17.5% and she contributes 3% to KiwiSaver. What is her approximate weekly take-home pay?
questions.explanation: Gross: 22 x 25 = $550. Tax: 550 x 17.5% = $96.25. KiwiSaver: 550 x 3% = $16.50. Take-home: 550 - 96.25 - 16.50 = approximately $437 (closest to $430-$468 range depending on ACC levy). Actual is around $437.

questions.question: Which income type does NOT require you to trade your time for money?
questions.explanation: Dividends are paid to shareholders from company profits — you receive money simply by owning shares, without working for it. This is passive income.

activities.title: Government support payments
activities.content:
Many young New Zealanders are entitled to government support:

**Student Allowance:** Up to ~$261/week for eligible full-time students (income and parent-tested)

**Working for Families:** Tax credits for families with children — can add hundreds per week

**Accommodation Supplement:** Helps with rent costs — up to $305/week depending on region and situation

**JobSeeker Support:** Income support if you cannot find work

**Student Loan:** Covers fees and living costs as a loan (not income — must be repaid)

Always check your entitlements at **workandincome.govt.nz** — many people miss payments they qualify for.
activities.notePrompt: Which of these support payments might you be eligible for currently or in the future?

questions.statement: You should budget using your gross (before tax) income.
questions.explanation: Always budget using net (take-home) income. Gross income includes tax you never receive — budgeting with it will leave you consistently short.

questions.statement: Your employer is required by law to contribute at least 3% to your KiwiSaver.
questions.explanation: As of 2013, all NZ employers must contribute a minimum of 3% of an employee's gross salary to their KiwiSaver — if the employee is enrolled.

questions.statement: Passive income requires ongoing daily effort to maintain.
questions.explanation: Passive income (dividends, rental income, interest) does not require daily effort once established. This is what makes it valuable — money working without your direct time input.

activities.instruction: Sort these income types as Active or Passive

activities.prompt: James works 30 hours/week at $18/hour. He also earns $40/week interest from a term deposit. His PAYE tax rate is 17.5% and he contributes 4% to KiwiSaver. Calculate his total weekly net income from all sources.
activities.sampleAnswer: Wages gross: 30 x $18 = $540. Tax on wages: $540 x 17.5% = $94.50. KiwiSaver: $540 x 4% = $21.60. Net wages: $540 - $94.50 - $21.60 = $423.90. Interest: $40 (taxed at RWT, approximately $40 x 17.5% = $7 tax deducted = $33 net). Total net income: approximately $423.90 + $33 = $456.90/week.
activities.hint: Calculate wages after tax and KiwiSaver separately, then add the after-tax interest income.
